root_cause_analysis.description: Ask 'Why?' repeatedly to drill down from symptom to root cause
root_cause_analysis.how_it_works: Start with the problem. Ask why it happened. Take that answer and ask why again. Repeat until you reach something actionable.

whys: Why? → Belt wasn't replaced during maintenance
Why? → I don't have a maintenance schedule
examples.solution: Create and follow a car maintenance schedule

whys: Why? → Didn't expect this much traffic
Why? → Marketing campaign went viral unexpectedly
Why? → No load testing before launch
examples.solution: Implement load testing as part of launch checklist

whys: Why? → Can't find things in the morning
Why? → Don't prepare the night before
Why? → Too tired in the evening
examples.solution: Set earlier bedtime, prepare clothes/bag at night

tips: Stop when you reach something you can control and change
Sometimes the root is 3 whys away, sometimes 7—it's not always exactly 5
Beware of blame—focus on processes, not people
If you get stuck, try asking 'What caused that?' instead of 'Why?'

substitution_logic.description: When you're missing an ingredient, understand its function to find the right substitute

substitutes.substitute: Flax egg (1 tbsp ground flax + 3 tbsp water)

substitutes.note: Makes things denser, sweeter—reduce sugar slightly
Adds banana flavor (good for banana bread!)

substitutes.substitute: Commercial egg replacer or aquafaba (3 tbsp per egg)
substitutes.note: Aquafaba (chickpea liquid) can even be whipped like egg whites

substitutes.ratio: Use 3/4 the amount (e.g., 3/4 cup oil for 1 cup butter)
substitutes.best_for: Muffins, cakes (makes them more moist but less rich)
substitutes.note: Coconut oil solidifies like butter, olive oil adds flavor
Oat milk is creamiest for baking, almond is neutral, coconut adds flavor

substitutes.substitute: Baking soda + acid (cream of tartar, lemon juice, vinegar)
substitutes.ratio: 1 tsp baking powder = 1/4 tsp baking soda + 1/2 tsp cream of tartar (or 1/2 tsp lemon juice/vinegar)
substitutes.note: Mix right before using—reaction happens immediately

substitutes.ratio: Use 3/4 cup liquid sweetener for 1 cup sugar, reduce other liquids by 3 tbsp
substitutes.note: Adds distinct flavor, makes things moister
Coconut sugar is less sweet, brown sugar adds molasses flavor

general_rules: Match the function: If eggs are for binding, use a binder (not a liquid)
Taste as you go: Substitutes often have distinct flavors
Expect texture changes: Substitutes rarely produce identical results—embrace the variation

systems_thinking_principles.description: Changes in one part of a system affect other parts
systems_thinking_principles.example: You start waking up earlier → More time for breakfast → Better energy → More productive at work → Less stress → Better sleep → Wake up earlier easier (positive feedback loop)
systems_thinking_principles.application: When solving a problem, ask: What else does this affect? What knock-on effects will my solution have?

systems_thinking_principles.description: Systems often have lag time between action and result
systems_thinking_principles.example: Start working out → Sore for weeks → No weight loss yet → Give up (didn't wait long enough for results)
systems_thinking_principles.application: Be patient with changes. Ask: How long until I see results? Don't quit during the delay.

systems_thinking_principles.description: Systems self-regulate through reinforcing or balancing loops

types.Reinforcing (amplify): Rich get richer—success breeds more success (or failure breeds more failure)
types.Balancing (stabilize): Thermostat—when temp rises, AC turns on to bring it back down

systems_thinking_principles.example:
Reinforcing: Procrastinate → More stress → Worse performance → More stress (vicious cycle)
Balancing: Eat too much → Feel full → Stop eating (homeostasis)

systems_thinking_principles.description: Small interventions in the right place can have huge impacts
systems_thinking_principles.example: Instead of treating individual symptoms (headaches, fatigue), address the root: poor sleep. Fixing sleep fixes everything downstream.
systems_thinking_principles.application: Find the bottleneck or root cause. Don't just treat symptoms.

steps.description: What exactly is the problem? Who is affected?
steps.example: Problem: Spending too much money. Define: Overspending by $300/month on dining out.
Track every dining expense for a month. Result: $450 on restaurants, $200 on coffee.

steps.description: Why is this happening? Use 5 Whys or root cause analysis.
steps.example: Why so much dining out? → Too tired to cook after work → Stay late at work → No morning routine → Oversleep → Poor sleep schedule.

steps.description: Implement a solution targeting the root cause.
steps.example: Solution: Set bedtime alarm, meal prep on Sundays, leave work at 5pm.

steps.description: Monitor to ensure the fix sticks. Create systems to prevent recurrence.
steps.example: Track spending monthly, set calendar reminder for meal prep, use app to track bedtime.

tips: When stuck, zoom out—look at the whole system, not just the immediate problem
Ask 'What if?' to explore unintended consequences before implementing solutions
Document your systems (morning routine, workflows, checklists) so they become automatic
Use checklists for recurring tasks to prevent errors (pilots do this for a reason)
Fix upstream problems—they're often easier and more impactful than downstream fixes
